Variant_1.question_text: Describe the motion of the skydiver between t = 0 and t = 20 s.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Acceptable answers: Award 1 mark for the skydiver accelerating or increasing speed. Award 1 mark for the skydiver having decreasing acceleration or the speed increasing at a decreasing rate or reaching a constant speed.

Variant_1.question_text: Explain the motion of the skydiver between t = 0 and t = 20 s in terms of the forces acting on him.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Acceptable answers: Initially, the acceleration is due to the force of gravity acting on the skydiver. As speed increases, air resistance increases until it balances the weight, resulting in zero net force and constant speed (terminal velocity). Award marks for: 1 mark for mentioning that gravity causes initial acceleration; 1 mark for explaining that air resistance increases and opposes gravity; 1 mark for stating that at terminal velocity, air resistance equals weight, leading to no resultant force.

Variant_1.question_text: Using Fig. 1.1, determine the terminal velocity of the skydiver. On Fig. 1.1, indicate any values used for your calculation.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Acceptable answers: The terminal velocity is calculated from the graph where the distance-time graph becomes a straight line, indicating constant speed. Award marks as follows: 1 mark for correctly identifying the straight-line portion (e.g., from t = 20 s onwards); 1 mark for calculating the gradient of that portion (change in distance divided by change in time); 1 mark for stating the terminal velocity as the value obtained, in appropriate units (m/s). For example, if the graph shows a rise of 1000 m over 20 s in the straight section, terminal velocity = 1000 m / 20 s = 50 m/s.

Variant_1.question_text: State how a scalar quantity differs from a vector quantity.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Acceptable answers: A scalar quantity has magnitude only, while a vector quantity has both magnitude and direction.

Variant_1.question_text: Underline every vector quantity in the list: distance, displacement, force, length, mass, time.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Acceptable answers: The vector quantities in the list are displacement and force. Award 1 mark for correctly underlining displacement and force only.

Variant_1.question_text: Explain how the motion of the satellite shows that a resultant force is acting on it.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Acceptable answers: The satellite moves in a circular path at constant speed, which requires a change in direction, indicating acceleration towards the center. This acceleration is caused by a resultant force. Award marks as follows: 1 mark for stating that the satellite's circular motion involves constant change in direction; 1 mark for explaining that this implies centripetal acceleration, due to a resultant force.

Variant_1.question_text: State the cause of this force.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Acceptable answers: The cause of the force is the gravitational attraction between the satellite and the Earth.

Variant_1.question_text: Calculate the moment of force P about the hinge.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Acceptable answers: The moment of force is calculated as force multiplied by perpendicular distance from the pivot. Force P is 25 N and the distance is 0.72 m. Moment = 25 N × 0.72 m = 18 N m. Award marks as follows: 1 mark for using the formula (force × distance); 1 mark for correct calculation and units.

Variant_1.question_text: Calculate the work done on the door by force P.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Acceptable answers: Work done is calculated as force multiplied by distance moved in the direction of the force. Force P is 25 N, and the door rotates 90°, so the distance is the arc length, which is the circumference of a quarter circle of radius 0.72 m. Circumference of full circle is 4.5 m, so quarter is 4.5 m / 4 = 1.125 m. Work done = 25 N × 1.125 m = 28.125 J. Award marks as follows: 1 mark for identifying the distance as the arc length (1.125 m); 1 mark for correct calculation and units.

Variant_1.question_text: Explain why the door rotates about the hinge even though force F is larger than force P.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Acceptable answers: The door rotates because the moment of force P about the hinge is greater than the moment of force F. This is due to the perpendicular distance of force P from the hinge being larger than that of force F, making P's turning effect greater. Award marks as follows: 1 mark for stating that rotation depends on the moment (force × distance); 1 mark for explaining that P has a larger moment despite F being larger in magnitude.

Variant_1.question_text: State what happens to the wavelength of the light in the ray as it enters the glass.

Variant_1.question_text: State what happens to the frequency of the light in the ray as it enters the glass.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Acceptable answers: The frequency of the light remains the same as it enters the glass from air.

Variant_1.question_text: Calculate the critical angle for light in glass.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Acceptable answers: The critical angle is calculated using the formula sin(c) = 1 / refractive index. Refractive index of glass is 1.6, so sin(c) = 1 / 1.6 = 0.625. Therefore, c = arcsin(0.625) ≈ 39°. Award marks as follows: 1 mark for using the correct formula; 1 mark for correct calculation and units (degrees).

Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for calculating the mass of the liquid: density multiplied by volume equals 1.8 g/cm3 times 200 cm3 equals 360 g. Award 1 mark for adding the mass of the beaker: 360 g plus 50 g equals 410 g. Acceptable answers include a final answer of 410 g or 0.41 kg.

Variant_2.question_text: State what is meant by centre of mass.

Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for calculating the resistance R for each arrangement using R = V / I and recording the values correctly in Table 2.1. Acceptable answers: For lamp X only: R = 1.4 V / 0.23 A ≈ 6.09 Ω; for lamp Y only: R = 1.3 V / 0.22 A ≈ 5.91 Ω; for lamps X and Y in parallel: R = 1.4 V / 0.45 A ≈ 3.11 Ω.

Variant_2.question_text: Use the results in Table 2.1 to suggest a correct conclusion about the combined resistance.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award up to 2 marks for suggesting a correct conclusion about the combined resistance of lamps X and Y in parallel. Award 1 mark for stating that the combined resistance is less than the individual resistances. Award 1 mark for explaining that it is equal to the reciprocal of the sum of reciprocals, based on the calculations. Acceptable answers: The combined resistance is approximately 3.11 Ω, which is less than the average of the individual resistances (about 6.00 Ω).

Variant_2.question_text: A student investigates reflection and refraction of light using a semi-circular glass block. He places a glass block on a sheet of paper. He draws around the outline of the block with a pencil. He uses a ray box to direct a ray of light onto the centre C of the flat face AB of the block. He labels AB and point C. Fig. 3.1 shows what the student observes. He uses a pencil to place two crosses X1 and X2 on the incident ray. He uses a pencil to place two crosses X3 and X4 on the emergent ray.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: No specific marking scheme provided in the given text, so this entry is based on general principles. Award marks for describing the observation or diagram as per the question requirements, but details are not available.

Variant_1.question_text: A list of various quantities is shown. acceleration displacement force length mass velocity How many of these quantities are vectors? A 2 B 3 C 4 D 5
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: C (4). The vector quantities in the list are acceleration, displacement, force, and velocity, making a total of 4 vectors.

Variant_1.question_text: Which instrument gives a reading that is the circumference of the football? A calipers B micrometer C rule D tape
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: D (tape). A tape measure is the appropriate instrument for measuring the circumference of a curved object like a football.

Variant_1.question_text: A train sets off from a station at time t = 0. The graph shows how the distance between the train and the station varies with time. Which statement about the movement of the train between time t1 and t2 is correct? A Its speed is decreasing and it is moving away from the station. B Its speed is decreasing and it is moving towards the station. C Its speed is increasing and it is moving away from the station. D Its speed is increasing and it is moving towards the station.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: D. This indicates that the train's speed is increasing and it is moving towards the station, as inferred from the graph where distance decreases while the slope becomes steeper.

Variant_1.question_text: A coin falls from rest through the air and eventually reaches a constant speed. There is a resultant force acting on the coin due to the two forces P and Q shown in the diagram. What happens to force P and what happens to the resultant force before the coin reaches constant speed? force P resultant force A decreases increases B decreases decreases C increases decreases D increases increases
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: C. Before the coin reaches constant speed, force P (air resistance) increases, and the resultant force decreases as it approaches zero when terminal velocity is reached.

Variant_1.question_text: A satellite is shown moving around the Earth in a circular path at a constant speed. Which arrow shows the direction of the force on the satellite?
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: D. The force on the satellite is the gravitational force towards the Earth, as indicated by arrow D in the diagram.

Variant_1.question_text: Which row shows the mass and the weight of an object on the Earth’s surface? [gravitational field strength g = 10 N / kg] mass / kg weight / N A 2 0.20 B 2 10 C 5 5.0 D 5 50
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: D. For a mass of 5 kg, the weight is mass times gravitational field strength, so 5 kg × 10 N/kg = 50 N.

Variant_1.question_text: Water is added to a measuring cylinder containing 100 cm3 of liquid paraffin. (The density of paraffin is 0.80 g / cm3 and that of the water is 1.0 g / cm3.) As the water is added, the level of the paraffin rises to 150 cm3. The paraffin and water do not mix. What finally is the total mass of liquid in the measuring cylinder? A 130 g B 140 g C 167 g D 175 g
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: A (130 g). The mass of paraffin is 100 cm3 × 0.80 g/cm3 = 80 g, and the volume of water added is 50 cm3, so its mass is 50 cm3 × 1.0 g/cm3 = 50 g, giving a total mass of 80 g + 50 g = 130 g.

Variant_1.question_text: A horizontal beam is pivoted at X. A mass of 200 g rests on the beam as shown. The centre of mass of the beam is 50 cm from the right-hand end of the beam. The beam is balanced. What is the mass of the beam? A 80 g B 100 g C 400 g D 800 g
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: C (400 g). For the beam to be balanced, the moments about the pivot must be equal: (200 g × 40 cm) + (mass of beam × 20 cm) = mass of beam × 70 cm, solving gives mass of beam = 400 g.

Variant_1.question_text: Where on the graph is the limit of proportionality for an elastic solid? A between O and P B at P C between P and Q D at Q
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: B (at P). The limit of proportionality is the point up to which the material obeys Hooke's law, which is at point P on the graph.

Variant_1.question_text: A sealed gas syringe contains a fixed mass of gas. The piston is moved and the volume of the gas doubles. The temperature of the gas does not change. What happens to the pressure of the gas? A halves B no change C doubles D triples
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: A (halves). According to Boyle's law, for a fixed mass of gas at constant temperature, if the volume doubles, the pressure halves.

Variant_1.question_text: Which expression for pressure is correct? A force × area B force ÷ area C mass × area D mass ÷ area
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: B (force ÷ area). Pressure is defined as force divided by the area over which it is applied.

Variant_1.question_text: At a depth d in sea-water, the total pressure experienced by a diver is 2P, where P is atmospheric pressure. At which depth is the pressure 4P? A 1.5d B 2d C 3d D 4d
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: C (3d). Pressure increases with depth due to the weight of the water; if pressure is 2P at depth d, then at 3d, it is 4P.

Variant_1.question_text: The work done by a force F on a body is calculated by multiplying F by a quantity q. What is q? A the distance travelled in the direction of the force B the distance travelled perpendicular to the direction of the force C the velocity in the direction of the force D the velocity in the direction perpendicular to the force
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: A. The quantity q is the distance travelled in the direction of the force, as work done is force multiplied by distance in the direction of the force.

Variant_1.question_text: Some solar panels have a total area of 12 m2. Each 1.0 m2 of the panels receives 0.85 kJ of energy from the Sun in 1.0 s. The efficiency of the panels is 16%. How much power do they produce? A 1.6 kW B 2.2 kW C 64 kW D 160 kW
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: A (1.6 kW). The total energy received is 12 m2 × 0.85 kJ/s = 10.2 kJ/s, and at 16% efficiency, the output power is 10.2 kJ/s × 0.16 = 1.632 kW, approximately 1.6 kW.

Variant_1.question_text: A copper rod is heated at one end. Which statement describes how heat transfer occurs in the copper? A Energetic copper molecules move from the cooler end to the hotter end. B Energetic copper molecules move from the hotter end to the cooler end. C Energetic free electrons move from the cooler end to the hotter end. D Energetic free electrons move from the hotter end to the cooler end.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: D. In metals like copper, heat is transferred by energetic free electrons moving from the hotter end to the cooler end.

Variant_1.question_text: Which factor affects the sensitivity of the thermometer? A the constriction B the diameter of the bore C the length of the glass tube D the thickness of the glass tube
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: B (the diameter of the bore). The sensitivity of a clinical thermometer is affected by the diameter of the bore, as a narrower bore allows for greater expansion visibility.

Variant_1.question_text: Which row is correct for a thermocouple thermometer? measures very high temperatures responds quickly to change in temperature A no no B no yes C yes no D yes yes
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: D. A thermocouple thermometer can measure very high temperatures and responds quickly to changes in temperature.

Variant_1.question_text: What is the heat capacity of a body? A the amount of thermal energy that the body can absorb without melting B the amount of thermal energy required to raise the temperature of the body by 1.0 °C C the amount of thermal energy required to raise the temperature of 1.0 kg of the body by 1.0 °C D the amount of thermal energy required to raise the temperature of 1.0 m3 of the body by 1.0 °C
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: B. Heat capacity is the amount of thermal energy required to raise the temperature of the entire body by 1.0 °C.

Variant_1.question_text: Which statement about water is correct? A At the boiling point, water vapour molecules have the same kinetic energy as liquid water molecules. B Evaporation occurs only at the boiling point. C Water molecules become heavier when water freezes. D Water molecules lose all of their kinetic energy when water freezes.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: A. At the boiling point, the average kinetic energy of water vapour molecules is the same as that of liquid water molecules at that temperature.

Variant_1.question_text: A water wave in a ripple tank refracts as it moves from deep water into shallower water. What happens to the speed and to the frequency
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: A. The speed of the wave decreases when it moves from deep to shallower water, but the frequency remains the same.

Variant_2.question_text: A student determines the circumference of a football. Which instrument gives a reading that is the circumference of the football? A calipers B micrometer C rule D tape
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting D: tape.

Variant_2.question_text: A coin falls from rest through the air and eventually reaches a constant speed. There is a resultant force acting on the coin due to the two forces P and Q shown in the diagram. What happens to force P and what happens to the resultant force before the coin reaches constant speed? force P resultant force A decreases increases B decreases decreases C increases decreases D increases increases
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting C: force P increases and resultant force decreases.

Variant_2.question_text: The diagrams show the forces acting on four identical solid blocks. Each arrow represents a force of 20 N. Which blocks are in equilibrium? A P and R only B Q and S only C P, Q and R only D P, Q, R and S
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting A: P and R only.

Variant_2.question_text: A student lists three changes that affect the stopping distance of a car. 1 increasing the braking force 2 increasing the friction between the tyres and the road 3 increasing the speed of the car Which change or changes increase the stopping distance? A 1 and 2 B 1 and 3 C 1 only D 3 only
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting D: 3 only.

Variant_2.question_text: The diagram shows a satellite orbiting a planet at a steady speed. In which direction does the resultant force act on the satellite?
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting A: towards the planet.

Variant_2.question_text: The gravitational field strength in space is smaller than on the Earth’s surface. A rocket is used to launch a satellite from the Earth’s surface into space. How are the mass and the weight of the satellite affected as the satellite moves away from the surface of the Earth and into space? A Both the mass and the weight are unaffected. B The mass decreases and the weight decreases. C The mass increases and the weight is unaffected. D The mass is unaffected and the weight decreases.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting D: The mass is unaffected and the weight decreases.

Variant_2.question_text: Air is trapped in a cylinder by a piston. The piston is moved inwards so that the volume of the air reduces. The density of the air in the syringe at the initial volume is 0.0012 g / cm3. What is the density of the air in the syringe at the final volume? A 0.0006 g / cm3 B 0.0012 g / cm3 C 0.0024 g / cm3 D 0.0048 g / cm3
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting C: 0.0024 g / cm3.

Variant_2.question_text: Some forces are applied at different distances from a pivot. Which diagram shows the force that produces the largest moment about the pivot?
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting B: the diagram with 30 N at 2.0 m from the pivot.

Variant_2.question_text: The diagram shows a children’s wooden play tower. Which change to the tower makes it more stable? A making the tower narrower B making the tower taller C lowering the centre of mass D raising the centre of mass
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting C: lowering the centre of mass.

Variant_2.question_text: Where on the graph is the limit of proportionality for an elastic solid?
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting B: at P.

Variant_2.question_text: A weight of 2.0 N is hung from a spring. The extension produced is 6.0 cm. The 2.0 N weight is removed and an 8.0 N weight is hung from the spring. The spring does not pass its limit of proportionality. What is the new extension of the spring? A 6.0 cm B 24 cm C 36 cm D 48 cm
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting B: 24 cm.

Variant_2.question_text: An object is placed at a depth d below the surface of a liquid of density ρ. On which expression does the pressure on the object depend? A d + ρ B d × ρ C d ÷ ρ D ρ ÷ d
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting B: d × ρ.

Variant_2.question_text: The diagram shows the dimensions of a box of mass M and weight W at rest on a table. What is the pressure on the table due to the box? A M xyz B M yz C W xyz D W yz
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting D: W yz.

Variant_2.question_text: Which graph shows the relationship between the pressure and the volume of a fixed mass of gas at constant temperature?
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting B: the graph showing pressure inversely proportional to volume.

Variant_2.question_text: The work done by a force F on a body is calculated by multiplying F by a quantity q. What is q? A the distance travelled in the direction of the force B the distance travelled perpendicular to the direction of the force C the velocity in the direction of the force D the velocity in the direction perpendicular to the force
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting A: the distance travelled in the direction of the force.

Variant_2.question_text: A girl lifts an object of mass 1.0 kg from the floor and puts it on a ledge 2.0 m above the floor. How much potential energy is gained by the object? [gravitational field strength g = 10 N / kg] A 2.0 J B 5.0 J C 10 J D 20 J
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting D: 20 J.

Variant_2.question_text: A copper rod is heated at one end. Which statement describes how heat transfer occurs in the copper? A Energetic copper molecules move from the cooler end to the hotter end. B Energetic copper molecules move from the hotter end to the cooler end. C Energetic free electrons move from the cooler end to the hotter end. D Energetic free electrons move from the hotter end to the cooler end.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting D: Energetic free electrons move from the hotter end to the cooler end.

Variant_2.question_text: Which heat transfer processes do not require a medium? A conduction only B convection only C radiation only D conduction and radiation
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting C: radiation only.

Variant_2.question_text: The list shows four physical properties. mass resistance voltage volume How many of these properties can be used to measure temperature? A 1 B 2 C 3 D 4
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting C: 3.

Variant_2.question_text: The diagram shows a clinical thermometer. Which factor affects the sensitivity of the thermometer? A the constriction B the diameter of the bore C the length of the glass tube D the thickness of the glass tube
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting B: the diameter of the bore.

Variant_1.question_text: An aircraft flies at a constant height. Air drag and the force from the aircraft’s engines together produce a force on the aircraft of 36 kN due north. The wind produces a force of 12 kN towards the east. Draw a scale drawing to show the resultant force acting on the aircraft. Use your drawing to determine the size of the resultant force and the angle between the resultant force and north. size of resultant force =  ....................................... angle =  .......................................
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for a scale drawing that accurately represents the 36 kN force north and 12 kN force east. Award 1 mark for correctly determining the magnitude of the resultant force (accept values between 30 kN and 40 kN, calculated as the vector sum). Award 1 mark for correctly determining the angle between the resultant force and north (accept values between 15° and 20°).

Variant_1.question_text: The acceleration of the aircraft is uniform. Describe how a uniform acceleration differs from a non-uniform acceleration.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for stating that uniform acceleration means the speed changes at a constant rate over time. Award 1 mark for stating that non-uniform acceleration means the speed changes at a varying rate over time. Acceptable answers: Uniform acceleration has a constant rate of change of velocity; non-uniform acceleration has a changing rate of change of velocity.

Variant_1.question_text: The mass of the aircraft is 60 000 kg. Calculate the acceleration of the aircraft.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for using the formula acceleration = resultant force / mass. Award 1 mark for correct calculation: resultant force from (a) divided by 60 000 kg, giving acceleration in m/s² (e.g., if resultant force is 36 kN, acceleration = 36 000 N / 60 000 kg = 0.6 m/s²).

Variant_1.question_text: Fig. 2.1 shows a hollow metal cube filled with boiling water. The temperature of the four vertical surfaces are equal but each surface has a different colour or texture. A thermal radiation sensor is placed the same distance from each surface and the meter reading measures the thermal radiation emitted from each surface. The results are shown in Fig. 2.2. Draw a line linking each type of surface with the appropriate meter reading. One line has been drawn for you.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for correctly linking dull black to 100, shiny black to 80, dull white to 60, and shiny white to 40, based on the meter's reading of thermal radiation. Not accepted: Any incorrect linking that does not match the expected order of radiation emission.

Variant_1.question_text: The hot surfaces produce a convection current in the air outside the metal cube. Describe how the convection current is produced.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for stating that the hot surfaces heat the air above them. Award 1 mark for explaining that the heated air expands and becomes less dense, rising upwards. Award 1 mark for stating that cooler, denser air moves in to replace the rising air, creating a circulation. Acceptable answers: The process involves the movement of air due to density differences caused by heating.

Variant_1.question_text: The steel rod has a mass of 2.0 kg and is initially at a temperature of 500 °C. It cools to 50 °C when placed in the water. The specific heat capacity of steel is 460 J / (kg °C). Calculate the thermal energy (heat) lost by the steel rod as it cools to 50 °C.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for using the formula thermal energy = mass × specific heat capacity × temperature change. Award 1 mark for correct substitution: 2.0 kg × 460 J/(kg °C) × (500 °C - 50 °C). Award 1 mark for the correct answer: 414 000 J or 4.14 × 10^5 J.

Variant_1.question_text: In both boiling and evaporation, water molecules escape into the air. State one way in which boiling is different from evaporation.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for stating that boiling occurs at a specific temperature (boiling point) throughout the liquid, while evaporation occurs at the surface at any temperature. Acceptable answers: Boiling produces bubbles of vapour within the liquid; evaporation does not.

Variant_1.question_text: The rate of evaporation decreases as the water cools. Explain why this happens, using ideas about molecules.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for stating that as water cools, the molecules have less kinetic energy. Award 1 mark for explaining that fewer molecules have enough energy to escape from the liquid surface into the air. Acceptable answers: At lower temperatures, molecules move slower, so the rate of molecules overcoming intermolecular forces decreases.

Award 1 mark for stating that Fig. 4.1 is a transverse wave. Award 1 mark for stating that Fig. 4.2 is a longitudinal wave. Acceptable answers: Transverse wave for Fig. 4.1 (where particles move perpendicular to the wave direction); longitudinal wave for Fig. 4.2 (where particles move parallel to the wave direction).

Variant_1.question_text: On Fig. 4.1, draw a wave which has a smaller frequency than the wave shown.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for drawing a wave on Fig. 4.1 with a longer wavelength or fewer waves in the same distance, indicating lower frequency.

Variant_1.question_text: Describe the movement of point X on the spring in Fig. 4.2 as the wave passes.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for stating that point X vibrates back and forth along the direction of the wave. Acceptable answers: The point oscillates parallel to the wave's travel direction.

Variant_2.question_text: State the difference between a scalar quantity and a vector quantity.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: a scalar quantity has magnitude only OR a vector quantity has both magnitude and direction.

Award up to 2 marks: For scalar quantities, award 1 mark for each of two correct examples, such as speed OR mass OR time OR distance OR energy. For vector quantities, award 1 mark for each of two correct examples, such as velocity OR force OR acceleration OR displacement OR weight. Acceptable answers include: speed (for scalar), velocity (for vector).

Variant_2.question_text: Fig. 1.1 shows the direction and size of two vectors P and Q. In the space next to Fig. 1.1, draw a labelled vector diagram to show the resultant vector obtained by adding vector P to vector Q. Draw vector P, vector Q and the resultant vector to the same scale as in Fig. 1.1.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award up to 2 marks: Award 1 mark for correctly drawing vector P and vector Q to the same scale as in Fig. 1.1. Award 1 mark for correctly drawing and labelling the resultant vector as the vector sum of P and Q, for example, by placing the tail of Q at the head of P and drawing the resultant from the tail of P to the head of Q.

Variant_2.question_text: The car starts to slow down a short time after the lights change to red. Determine the time between the lights changing to red and the car starting to slow down.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: identifying the time from the graph as 1 second (from t=0 to t=1 s, based on the graph description).

Variant_2.question_text: State what is meant by uniform acceleration.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: uniform acceleration means that the velocity changes at a constant rate over time OR the acceleration is constant.

Variant_2.question_text: State how Fig. 2.1 shows that the deceleration of the car between t = 2 s and t = 7 s is non-uniform.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: the graph of speed against time is not a straight line between t=2 s and t=7 s, indicating that the slope (rate of change of speed) is changing.

Variant_2.question_text: Determine the distance the car travels from the moment the car starts to slow down until it stops.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award up to 3 marks for the calculation: Award 1 mark for identifying the area under the graph from t=1 s to t=8 s (when speed reaches 0). Award 1 mark for calculating the area (e.g., trapezoid or average speed method). Award 1 mark for the correct distance, such as 45 m (based on graph data).

Variant_2.question_text: Fig. 3.1 shows a small brick hanging from a newton meter. The reading on the newton meter is 3.0 N. Describe how the reading on the newton meter is used to find the mass of the brick.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: using the reading on the newton meter (force in newtons) and dividing by the acceleration due to gravity (approximately 10 m/s²) to find mass, e.g., mass = force / g.

Variant_2.question_text: State the principle of moments for a body in equilibrium.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: for a body in equilibrium, the sum of the clockwise moments about any point equals the sum of the anticlockwise moments about that same point.

Variant_2.question_text: Determine the reading on the newton meter shown in Fig. 3.2.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award up to 2 marks: Award 1 mark for applying the principle of moments (e.g., moments about the pivot: brick moment = meter moment). Award 1 mark for the correct reading, such as 6.0 N (based on balance with 20 cm and 10 cm distances).

Variant_2.question_text: A beaker of water is placed so that the brick is partly submerged in the water, as shown in Fig. 3.3. The apparatus is adjusted to keep the rule horizontal. Suggest why the reading on the newton meter is less than your answer in (b)(ii).
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award up to 2 marks: Award 1 mark for: the buoyant force or upthrust on the brick reduces the effective weight. Award 1 mark for: the reading is less because the water provides an upward force equal to the weight of the displaced water.

Variant_2.question_text: On Fig. 4.1, draw tube B in the position where the sound heard by the student is loudest.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: drawing tube B in a position that reflects the sound wave, such as along the line of reflection from the barrier.

Variant_2.question_text: Explain your answer to (i).
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award up to 1 mark (based on context): Award 1 mark for: the sound is loudest when tube B is placed along the path of the reflected ray, as this is where the reflected wave interferes constructively.

Variant_1.question_text: On Fig. 1.1: switch on the lamp or ray box and position the slit at the top of the page so that a single ray of light lines up with the line A A; place the block on Fig. 1.1 and line up the corner of the block with the corner at B so that the ray of light passes through the long edge of the block at the point labelled C; mark with the letter D the point where the refracted ray emerges from the other side of the block; mark with the letter E the point where this refracted ray passes through line F F; draw a line from point D to point E; mark with the letter G the point where the reflected ray from point C passes through line F F; draw a line from point C to point G.

Variant_1.question_text: Measure and record the distance d between point E and point G.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for correctly measuring and recording the distance d between points E and G on Fig. 1.1, with the value recorded in centimetres and to an appropriate level of accuracy.

Variant_1.question_text: On Fig. 1.1, draw the normal to the surface of the block at point C and label with an i the angle of incidence of the ray of light with the block. Measure and record the angle of incidence i.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for correctly drawing the normal at point C on Fig. 1.1 and labelling the angle of incidence as i. Award 1 more mark for accurately measuring and recording the angle i in degrees, ensuring it is the angle between the incident ray and the normal.

Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for describing the relationship between the angle of incidence i and the distance d, such as stating that as i increases, d increases, based on observations of the refracted and reflected rays passing through line F F.

Variant_1.question_text: Close the switch and record the reading on the voltmeter.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for correctly recording the voltmeter reading in volts, ensuring the value is as observed from the circuit and includes the appropriate unit.

Variant_1.question_text: One component in the circuit is resting on the piece of card labelled X. Carefully move your hand over the surface of this component. State and explain what happens to the component on the piece of card labelled Y.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for stating and explaining the effect on the component on the piece of card labelled Y, such as it heating up or moving, based on the interaction with the component on card X, ensuring the explanation is clear and relates to the circuit's behaviour.

Variant_1.question_text: Using some or all of the symbols in Table 2.1, draw a circuit diagram of the circuit.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for correctly using the symbols from Table 2.1 to draw the circuit diagram, including all components like the battery, switch, ammeter, voltmeter, and other elements as per the setup. Award 1 more mark for accurate connections and layout. Award 1 more mark for including all necessary symbols and ensuring the diagram is complete.

Variant_1.question_text: Describe how you ensured an accurate measurement of h.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for describing a method to ensure accurate measurement of h, such as using a metre rule aligned properly and checking for perpendicular alignment with the set-square. Award 1 more mark for detailing steps like ensuring the mass hanger is at rest and the clamp is adjusted precisely.

Variant_1.question_text: Carefully place the piece of modelling clay on mass hanger A and hold this mass hanger in position. Release the mass hanger and start the stop-watch. Measure and record an accurate value for the time t for the mass hanger to fall to the floor.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for accurately measuring and recording the time t in seconds for the mass hanger to fall, ensuring the measurement is taken from release to hitting the floor.

Variant_1.question_text: The mass m of the piece of modelling clay is written on the piece of card. Record the total mass M in kilograms of the mass hanger and the modelling clay.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for correctly recording the total mass M in kilograms, adding the mass of the hanger and the modelling clay as provided.

Variant_1.question_text: Calculate the power P produced as mass hanger A falls, using the equation: P = Mgh / t
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for correctly substituting values into the equation P = (M × g × h) / t, where M is the total mass in kilograms, g is the acceleration due to gravity (approximately 10 m/s² or as specified), h is the height in metres, and t is the time in seconds, and calculating the power P in watts. Ensure units are included and the calculation is shown.

Variant_2.question_text: In this experiment you will investigate reflection and refraction of light. You are provided with a transparent block, an illuminated slit or ray box, a ruler and a protractor. On Fig. 1.1: switch on the lamp or ray box and position the slit at the top of the page so that a single ray of light lines up with the line A A; place the block on Fig. 1.1 and line up the corner of the block with the corner at B so that the ray of light passes through the long edge of the block at the point labelled C; mark with the letter D the point where the refracted ray emerges from the other side of the block; mark with the letter E the point where this refracted ray passes through line F F; draw a line from point D to point E; mark with the letter G the point where the reflected ray from point C passes through line F F; draw a line from point C to point G.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for correctly drawing lines from the point marked D to the point marked E and from point C to point G, ensuring that points C and G are on line F F, and that the diagram appears accurate by eye.

Variant_2.question_text: Measure and record the distance d between point E and point G.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for correctly measuring the length of the line segment from the candidate's marked point E to their marked point G on line F F.

Variant_2.question_text: Remove the block. On Fig. 1.1, draw the normal to the surface of the block at point C and label with an i the angle of incidence of the ray of light with the block. Measure and record the angle of incidence i.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for drawing a normal line through point C that extends on both sides of the interface and correctly labelling the angle of incidence as i. Award the second mark for accurately measuring and recording the angle of incidence i in degrees.

Variant_2.question_text: Place the block again on page 3 with its corner lined up at corner B. Carefully rotate the long edge of the block about the point C and observe how this changes the position of the refracted and reflected rays that pass through F F. Switch off the lamp. Describe the relationship between i and d.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for a description that correctly states the relationship between the angle of incidence i and the distance d, such as that as i increases, d changes in a specific way (e.g., increases or decreases), based on observations of the refracted and reflected rays.

Variant_2.question_text: In this experiment you will investigate an electrical circuit. You are provided with components connected together in an electrical circuit, an ammeter and a voltmeter. The supervisor has set up the circuit. Close the switch and record the reading on the voltmeter.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for accurately recording the voltmeter reading in volts, as observed from the circuit.

Variant_2.question_text: One component in the circuit is resting on the piece of card labelled X. Carefully move your hand over the surface of this component. State and explain what happens to the component on the piece of card labelled Y. Open the switch.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for stating and explaining the effect on the component on the piece of card labelled Y, such as it heats up or moves, due to the electrical current or heat from the component on X.

Variant_2.question_text: Some electrical symbols are shown in Table 2.1. Using some or all of the symbols in Table 2.1, draw a circuit diagram of the circuit.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for correctly using the appropriate symbols from Table 2.1 to represent the components in the circuit. Award the second mark for accurately connecting the symbols to show the circuit layout. Award the third mark for including all necessary elements, such as the ammeter, voltmeter, battery, switch, and other components, ensuring the diagram is complete and correct.

Variant_2.question_text: In this experiment you will investigate the power developed by a falling mass. You are provided with a pulley attached to a stand, two 100 g mass hangers, a length of string, a metre rule, a stop-watch, a set-square and a heavy weight or G-clamp to keep stand in position. The supervisor has set up the apparatus as shown in Fig. 3.1. Adjust the position of the clamp so that h = 1.000 m and mass hanger B is at rest on the floor. Describe how you ensured an accurate measurement of h.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for describing a method to ensure accuracy, such as using a set-square to align the metre rule perpendicularly or checking the measurement at eye level to avoid parallax error. Award the second mark for a complete description that includes using the metre rule to measure from the floor to the appropriate point and verifying the distance.

Variant_2.question_text: You are also provided with a small piece of modelling clay. Carefully place the piece of modelling clay on mass hanger A and hold this mass hanger in position. Release the mass hanger and start the stop-watch. Measure and record an accurate value for the time t for the mass hanger to fall to the floor.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for accurately measuring and recording the time t in seconds for the mass hanger to fall to the floor using the stop-watch.

Variant_2.question_text: The mass m of the piece of modelling clay is written on the piece of card. Record the total mass M in kilograms of the mass hanger and the modelling clay.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for correctly recording the total mass M in kilograms, which is the sum of the mass of the mass hanger and the mass of the modelling clay.

Variant_2.question_text: Calculate the power P produced as mass hanger A falls, using the equation: P = mgh / t
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for substituting the correct values into the equation P = mgh / t, where m is the total mass M, g is 10 m/s² (or the value used in the context), h is 1.000 m, and t is the measured time. Award the second mark for the correct calculation and final answer with appropriate units.

Variant_1.question_text: State one property of the wire that must be the same for the student to make a fair comparison.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: thickness, diameter, or cross-sectional area.

Variant_1.question_text: On Fig. 1.1, draw a voltmeter symbol connected to measure the voltage across the length of wire.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for drawing a voltmeter symbol correctly connected in parallel with the wire.

Variant_1.question_text: Name the piece of apparatus he uses to measure the length of the wire between the crocodile clips.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for: metre rule, measuring tape, or half-metre rule.

Variant_1.question_text: Record the readings of current and voltage for this length of wire. Current = ... mA, Voltage = ... V
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for the current reading of 110.0 mA. Award 1 mark for the voltage reading of 0.68 V.

Variant_1.question_text: Complete Table 1.1, using your recorded readings in (c)(ii).
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark if all values in Table 1.1 are correct, including the voltage of 0.68 V for the 40.0 cm length, and the corresponding current and resistance values calculated accurately.

Variant_1.question_text: On Fig. 1.3, plot the graph of R / Ω on the y-axis against l / cm on the x-axis. Start both axes from the origin (0,0). Draw the straight line of best fit.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award up to 4 marks for: plotting points accurately (up to 2 marks), drawing axes with appropriate scales starting from (0,0) (1 mark), and drawing a straight line of best fit through the points (1 mark).

Variant_1.question_text: State the relationship between R and l shown by your graph.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for stating that resistance R is directly proportional to length l.

Variant_1.question_text: Use your graph to determine the length of wire that has a resistance of 14 Ω. Show how you determine this value on your graph. Length of wire needed = ... cm
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for correctly determining the length from the graph for a resistance of 14 Ω, ensuring the value is read accurately from the line of best fit.

Variant_1.question_text: Suggest a reason for waiting a short time.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for suggesting that waiting allows the wire to cool down, preventing overheating which could affect resistance or ensure accurate readings.

Variant_1.question_text: On Fig. 2.1, measure, to the nearest millimetre, the length x and the length y. x = ... cm, y = ... cm
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for accurately measuring and recording the lengths x and y to the nearest millimetre as shown in Fig. 2.1.

Variant_1.question_text: Fig. 2.1 is drawn to one quarter full size. Calculate the actual distance u between the lens and the object and calculate the actual distance v between the lens and the screen. u = ... cm, v = ... cm
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for correctly calculating the actual distances by multiplying the measured values by 4, as the figure is drawn to one quarter full size.

Variant_1.question_text: Calculate the focal length f of the lens using the equation: f = uv / (u + v). Give your answer to 2 significant figures. f = ... cm
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for substituting the values of u and v into the equation and calculating f correctly. Award 1 mark for giving the answer to 2 significant figures.

Variant_1.question_text: Explain, giving a reason, whether your results agree with the manufacturer’s value of f = 7.0 cm within the limits of experimental accuracy.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for explaining whether the calculated focal length agrees with 7.0 cm, providing a reason based on experimental error or measurement accuracy.

Variant_1.question_text: Suggest why the student waits a short time before recording the temperature at time t = 0.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for suggesting that waiting allows the water to stabilise or reach a uniform temperature, ensuring an accurate initial reading.

Variant_2.question_text: Read the voltmeter scale and record the p.d. V in Table 1.1.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for recording the potential difference as 1.40 V.

Variant_2.question_text: Calculate 1/V and record its value in Table 1.1 to the appropriate number of significant figures.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for correctly calculating 1 divided by the potential difference V (using V = 1.40 V, so 1 / 1.40 = 0.71428...). Award 1 mark for the value 0.71, which is to two significant figures.

Variant_2.question_text: Suggest why the student opens the switch between readings.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for any of the following acceptable answers: the cell runs down, the wire gets hot, the cell gets hot, or the resistor gets hot.

Variant_2.question_text: On the grid provided in Fig. 1.3, plot a graph of 1/V on the y-axis against l on the x-axis. Start both axes from the origin (0, 0). Draw the best-fit straight line.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for axes labelled with quantity and unit and oriented correctly. Award 1 mark for scales that are linear, not awkward, and start from (0,0). Award 1 mark for points plotted accurately. Award 1 mark for drawing the best-fit straight line.

Variant_2.question_text: Calculate the gradient m of your line. Show all working and indicate on the graph the values you use.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for showing all working in the calculation of the gradient. Award 1 mark for the correct value of the gradient.

Variant_2.question_text: Extend your line until it crosses the y-axis. Measure the intercept c that your line makes with the y-axis.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for correctly measuring and recording the y-intercept value.

Variant_2.question_text: Calculate the electromotive force (e.m.f.) E of the cell using the equation: E = k / c where k = 1.0 V.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for substituting the values into the equation and calculating E correctly, where E = 1.0 / c.

Variant_2.question_text: Suggest one practical reason why, despite carrying out the experiment with care, the student’s value for E may not be the true value.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for suggesting a reason such as: the cell running down, heating effects in the wire or resistor, or inaccurate measurement of lengths or voltages.

Variant_2.question_text: State how the results in Table 1.1 show that l is not inversely proportional to V.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for stating that if l were inversely proportional to V, then 1/V should be directly proportional to l, but the graph or data does not show a straight line through the origin or the expected linear relationship exactly.

Variant_2.question_text: Suggest a measuring device that the student uses to ensure that AB and CD are 120.0 cm apart.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for suggesting a device such as a ruler, metre rule, or tape measure.

Variant_2.question_text: Suggest a measuring device that the student uses to ensure that the ramp makes an angle of 30° with the bench.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for suggesting a device such as a protractor or inclinometer.

Variant_2.question_text: Suggest one reason why there is a large variation in these times.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for suggesting reasons such as friction varying, the trolley not starting from rest properly, or human reaction time error in starting/stopping the stopwatch.

Variant_2.question_text: On Fig. 2.2, draw the position of the trolley when it has travelled 120.0 cm.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for accurately drawing the trolley at the position corresponding to line CD on the ramp.

Variant_2.question_text: Calculate the average time tAV for the trolley to travel a distance d = 120.0 cm.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for calculating the average of the times 0.70 s, 0.81 s, and 0.95 s, which is (0.70 + 0.81 + 0.95) / 3 = 0.82 s.

Variant_2.question_text: Calculate the acceleration a of the trolley down the ramp using the equation: a = 2d / tAV^2. Give the unit of your answer.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for substituting values into the equation (d = 120.0 cm = 1.20 m, tAV = 0.82 s) to get a = 2 × 1.20 / (0.82)^2. Award 1 mark for the correct value and unit, approximately 2.85 m/s^2.

Variant_2.question_text: Suggest how the student could modify his experiment so that a more accurate value for the acceleration a of the trolley is obtained.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for suggestions such as using a smoother ramp to reduce friction, using light gates or electronic timers for more precise time measurement, or repeating the experiment more times and taking a better average.

Variant_1.question_text: Which quantity is a vector?
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: B, which is force.

Variant_1.question_text: Two forces, X and Y, act upon an object O. The arrows represent the magnitudes and directions of the forces. Which arrow shows the direction of the resultant force?
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: A, which is the arrow showing the direction of the resultant force as indicated in the diagram.

Variant_1.question_text: Two identical objects begin to fall from rest. One object falls from 200 m above the Earth’s surface and the other falls from 200 m above the Moon’s surface. One second after they have started to fall, both objects are still accelerating. There is no atmosphere on the Moon and the weight of each object is constant. Which row describes the motion of both objects at this time?
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: A, which states that the acceleration of the object falling above the Earth’s surface is decreasing and the acceleration of the object falling above the Moon’s surface is constant.

Variant_1.question_text: The diagram shows a car going around a circular track at constant speed. Which arrow shows the direction of the resultant force on the car?
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: D, which is the arrow showing the direction of the resultant force as indicated in the diagram.

Variant_1.question_text: An electron has mass. Where does a stationary electron experience a force?
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: C, which is in a gravitational field and an electric field only.

Variant_1.question_text: A stone is placed in a measuring cylinder of water. The volume of water in the cylinder before adding the stone is 15 cm³ and the stone has a mass of 90 g. What is the density of the stone?
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: C, which is 1.5 g / cm³.

Variant_1.question_text: The extension of a spring is measured for various loads. The limit of proportionality is not reached. Which graph shows the extension–load graph for the spring?
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: B, which is the graph showing a straight line from the origin up to a certain point and then curving upwards.

Variant_1.question_text: An underwater diver moves from the ocean to a fresh water lake. The density of water in the lake is less than in the ocean. In which position does the diver experience the smallest pressure due to the water?
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: C, which is the position in the lake as shown in the diagram.

Variant_1.question_text: Air is trapped in a cylinder by a piston. The pressure of the air is p and the length of the air column is 20 cm. The piston is moved outwards. The length of the air column increases by 40 cm. The temperature of the air remains constant. What is the new air pressure?
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: B, which is 3p (one-third of the original pressure p).

Variant_1.question_text: When the pressure exerted by the atmosphere is 0.10 MPa, the height of the mercury in a barometer is 0.76 m. What is the pressure exerted by the atmosphere when the height of the mercury in the simple barometer is 0.57 m?
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: B, which is 0.075 MPa.

Variant_1.question_text: The diagram shows a block of mass m pulled in a straight line along a horizontal surface by a force F. The block moves a distance d in time t. The average speed at which the block moves is v. Which two quantities must be known to calculate the work done?
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: A, which is F and d.

Variant_1.question_text: The diagram shows the inside of a refrigerator. When the refrigerator is first switched on, what happens to the air near the cooling unit?
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: D, which states that the molecules of this air move closer together and the density of this air increases.

Variant_1.question_text: Less thermal energy is needed to raise the temperature of 1.0 kg of copper by 1.0 °C than is needed to raise the temperature of 1.0 kg of water by 1.0 °C. Which statement explains this?
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: B, which is copper has a lower specific heat capacity.

Variant_1.question_text: The graph is the temperature–time graph for a sample of wax that is heated so that it melts. The mass of the wax is 200 g. Thermal energy is supplied to the wax at a constant rate of 12 000 J / minute. What is the specific latent heat of fusion of the wax?
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: B, which is 240 J / g.

Variant_1.question_text: A fixed amount of gas is trapped inside a metal can. The temperature of the gas decreases but the volume does not change. How does this change affect the kinetic energy of the gas molecules and the pressure inside the metal can?
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: A, which states that the kinetic energy decreases and the pressure decreases.

Variant_1.question_text: The diagram shows a room temperature liquid in a beaker. What reduces the rate of loss of liquid by evaporation?
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: C, which is putting a lid on the beaker.

Variant_1.question_text: A water wave on a pond passes into a region where the wave travels more slowly. It then returns to a region where it travels at the original speed. Assume that the frequency of the wave stays constant. Which side-view of the wave is correct?
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: A, which is the side-view showing the wave with increased wavelength in the slower region.

Variant_1.question_text: What is meant by the amplitude of a wave?
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: B, which is the maximum distance a particle moves from its equilibrium position.

Variant_2.question_text: A boy starts at P and walks 3.0 m due north from P to Q and then 4.0 m due east from Q to R. What is the shortest distance that he must now walk to have an overall displacement of zero?
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: C, which is 5.0 m. This is the magnitude of the vector from R back to P, calculated using the Pythagorean theorem for the right-angled triangle formed by the paths.

Variant_2.question_text: Which quantity is a vector?
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: B, which is the gravitational force on the ball. A vector quantity has both magnitude and direction, unlike scalar quantities such as distance or speed.

Variant_2.question_text: A skier slides down a slope. The diagram shows how his speed varies with time. What is his average acceleration during the 6.0 s?
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: B, which is 4.3 m / s². Average acceleration is calculated as the change in speed divided by the time taken, using the graph data from 0 to 6.0 s.

Variant_2.question_text: A solid object, immersed in water, hangs from an elastic thread. Three forces act on the object: its weight W, the tension in the thread T, and a force F from the water. The force F acts upwards. Which equation is correct when the object is stationary?
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: D, which is F – W + T = 0. When the object is stationary, the net force is zero, so the upward force F and tension T balance the downward weight W.

Variant_2.question_text: The diagram shows a car going around a circular track at constant speed. Which arrow shows the direction of the resultant force on the car?
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: D. The resultant force is the centripetal force, directed towards the center of the circular path.

Variant_2.question_text: An object in a laboratory is used to determine the gravitational field strength g. Which quantity is equal to g?
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: C, which is the weight divided by the mass of the object. Gravitational field strength g is defined as the acceleration due to gravity, equal to weight per unit mass.

Variant_2.question_text: A student takes measurements to find the density of a regularly shaped block. Which formula is used to calculate the density of the block?
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: A, which is density equals mass divided by volume. Density is defined as mass per unit volume.

Variant_2.question_text: Four solid objects are made from the same material and have equal volumes. Which object is the most stable?
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: D. Stability depends on the base area and center of gravity; the object with the widest base and lowest center of gravity is the most stable.

Variant_2.question_text: A metal wire is stretched by a force F up to the limit of proportionality. Which statement describes what happens when F is increased slightly?
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: B, which is the wire continues to extend but not in direct proportion to F. Beyond the limit of proportionality, the extension is no longer proportional to the force.

Variant_2.question_text: Piston X is pushed into a hydraulic cylinder. Piston X produces a pressure PX in the liquid in the cylinder. There are two other pistons, Y and Z, in the cylinder. The pressures on piston Y and Z are PY and PZ. What is the relationship between PX, PY and PZ?
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: D, which is PX equals PY equals PZ. In a hydraulic system, pressure is transmitted equally throughout the fluid.

Variant_2.question_text: Air is trapped in a cylinder by a piston. The pressure of the air is p and the length of the air column is 20 cm. The piston is moved outwards. The length of the air column increases by 40 cm. The temperature of the air remains constant. What is the new air pressure?
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: B, which is p divided by 3. According to Boyle's law, for constant temperature, pressure is inversely proportional to volume; the volume triples, so pressure becomes one-third.

Variant_2.question_text: A 60 kg ice-skater, travelling initially at 3.0 m / s, glides across a horizontal icy surface. The skater brakes and comes to rest after 5.0 m. The work done against the braking force is the change in the kinetic energy of the skater. What is the braking force?
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: C, which is 54 N. The braking force is calculated using the work-energy principle: work done equals change in kinetic energy, and work equals force times distance.

Variant_2.question_text: Which equation is used to find the efficiency of an energy transfer?
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: A, which is efficiency equals useful energy output divided by total energy input. Efficiency is the ratio of useful output to total input.

Variant_2.question_text: The diagram shows the inside of a refrigerator. When the refrigerator is first switched on, what happens to the air near the cooling unit?
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: D, which is the molecules of this air move closer together and the density of this air increases. Cooling causes the air molecules to lose kinetic energy and contract, increasing density.

Variant_2.question_text: What is the colour and what is the texture of a good absorber of infrared radiation?
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: B, which is black and dull. A black, dull surface absorbs infrared radiation effectively due to its low reflectivity.

Variant_2.question_text: Which temperatures are used as the lower and upper fixed points for a mercury-in-glass thermometer?
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: B, which is freezing point of water and boiling point of water. These are the standard fixed points at 0°C and 100°C under normal conditions.

Variant_2.question_text: Two liquid-in-glass thermometers have identical dimensions. One contains ethanol and the other an equal volume of mercury. Ethanol expands more than mercury for the same temperature rise. How do the sensitivity and range of the ethanol thermometer compare to those of the mercury thermometer?
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: B, which is greater sensitivity and smaller range for the ethanol thermometer. Greater expansion means higher sensitivity, but ethanol's lower boiling point limits the range.

Variant_2.question_text: Solar panels are used to heat water with a mass of 500 kg. The total area of the solar panels is 10 m² and the average power output from each square metre of the panels is 300 W. The specific heat capacity of water is 4200 J / (kg °C). What is the increase in the water temperature after 8.0 hours? (Assume there is no heat loss.)
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: D, which is 41 °C. The energy supplied is calculated from power and time, then used in the formula for heat energy to find the temperature rise.

Variant_2.question_text: The graph is the temperature–time graph for a sample
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for selecting the correct option: B. The question is incomplete in the provided text, but based on the mark scheme, this refers to interpreting a temperature-time graph, likely involving phase changes or heating/cooling processes.

Variant_1.question_text: Each of the two supports has an area of 0.040 m² in contact with the ground. Calculate the pressure on the ground due to the bench.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for using the formula pressure = force / area, for example, force of 2000 N divided by total area of 2 × 0.040 m², which gives an intermediate value of 50 000 or 25000 Pa. Award 1 mark for the correct final answer of 25000 Pa.

Variant_1.question_text: Suggest one reason why the centre of mass of the bench is 1.1 m from the left-hand end of the bench and 0.24 m from the front.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for stating that the bench is symmetrical left-to-right or symmetrical front-to-back or that the bench is stable. Award 1 mark for explaining that this is because the centre of mass is in the middle horizontally.

Variant_2.question_text: State two other ways in which weight differs from mass.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for each of the following points, up to 2 marks: - Weight is a vector quantity and has a direction. - Weight is a force. - Weight depends on the location or the gravitational field strength. - Weight is measured with a spring balance, not a beam balance.

Variant_1.question_text: Measure and record angle a on Fig. 1.1. angle a = ......... °
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for accurately measuring and recording the angle a from the diagram. Acceptable answers: The measured value should correspond to the angle shown in the diagram, typically between 0° and 90°, but exact value depends on the diagram provided.

Variant_1.question_text: On Fig. 1.1: place the illuminated slit plate or the slit plate of the ray box on the line PP and adjust so that a single ray of light lines up with the centre line; place the pair of mirrors so that the corner is at point C and one mirror lines up with the marked area; switch on the lamp; observe the ray reflected from mirror 1, adjust mirror 2 so that the ray reflects from it, rotate mirror 2 so that the reflected ray passes through point X, draw a line along the face of mirror 2 and label it X; measure and record angle bX between this line and mirror 1. angle bX = ......... °
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for correctly measuring and recording angle bX after following the procedure. Acceptable answers: The angle bX should be measured accurately as the angle between the line drawn along mirror 2 and mirror 1, typically ensuring the ray passes through point X as described.

Variant_1.question_text: Repeat the procedure in (b)(i) for points Y and Z, labelling the lines Y and Z respectively. angle bY = ......... ° angle bZ = ......... °
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for correctly measuring and recording both angles bY and bZ after repeating the procedure for points Y and Z. Acceptable answers: Both angles should be measured accurately, with bY for point Y and bZ for point Z, ensuring the rays pass through the respective points as described.

Variant_1.question_text: Describe the trend in angle b as the reflected ray moves from point X to point Z.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for correctly describing the trend in angle b. Acceptable answers: The angle b increases as the reflected ray moves from point X to point Z, indicating that the angle between the mirrors changes progressively.

Variant_1.question_text: Suggest one practical reason why it is difficult to obtain accurate results with this experiment.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for suggesting a valid practical reason. Acceptable answers: Possible reasons include difficulty in precisely aligning the mirrors, inaccuracies in drawing the reflected ray lines, or variations in the light ray due to environmental factors such as room lighting or mirror surface imperfections.

Variant_1.question_text: Using some or all of the symbols in Fig. 2.1, draw a circuit diagram of the circuit.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award up to 3 marks for an accurate circuit diagram. Award 1 mark for including the battery of cells; award 1 mark for including the ammeter in series; award 1 mark for including the voltmeter in parallel and other components as described, such as the lamp, switch, and resistors. Acceptable answers: The diagram must use the provided symbols correctly and show the circuit as set up by the supervisor, ensuring proper connections.

Variant_1.question_text: Close the switch and record the readings on the ammeter and the voltmeter. State the unit of each reading. ammeter reading = ......... unit ......... voltmeter reading = ......... unit .........
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for correctly recording the ammeter and voltmeter readings with appropriate units. Acceptable answers: The readings should match the actual circuit values, with units such as amperes for current and volts for voltage; for example, ammeter reading in A and voltmeter reading in V.

Variant_1.question_text: Describe how you can obtain steady voltmeter readings.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for describing a method to obtain steady readings. Acceptable answers: Ensure the circuit is stable by waiting for the components to reach equilibrium, such as allowing time for the lamp to warm up or using a stable power source, and avoiding fluctuations in the circuit.

Variant_1.question_text: Let the ball roll from the top of the slope at the 100 cm mark to the bottom of the slope at the 0 cm mark. Determine an accurate average time t1 for the ball to roll from the top of the slope to the half-way point at the 50 cm mark on the rule. t1 = ......... s
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for accurately determining and recording the average time t1. Acceptable answers: The time should be measured using the stop-watch for multiple trials and averaged, typically in seconds, for the ball rolling from 100 cm to 50 cm.

Variant_1.question_text: With the ball continuing to start its roll from the top of the slope, now determine an accurate average time t2 for the ball to roll from the half-way point at the 50 cm mark to the bottom of the slope at the 0 cm mark. t2 = ......... s
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for accurately determining and recording the average time t2. Acceptable answers: The time should be measured for the ball rolling from 50 cm to 0 cm, using multiple trials and averaging, in seconds.

Variant_1.question_text: Calculate the average speed v1 of the ball in the top half of the slope and the average speed v2 of the ball in the bottom half of the slope.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award up to 2 marks for correct calculations (as part of the total for Question 3). Award 1 mark for calculating v1 = distance (50 cm) / t1, with appropriate units; award 1 mark for calculating v2 = distance (50 cm) / t2, with appropriate units. Acceptable answers: Speeds should be in m/s or cm/s, showing working and correct significant figures.

Variant_2.question_text: Measure and record angle a on Fig. 1.1.
Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for the angle measured as 85° with a tolerance of ±1°, meaning answers of 84°, 85°, or 86° are acceptable.

Variant_2.question_text: Describe how you could investigate the relationship between angle a and the distance d.

Variant_2.marking_scheme: Award 2 marks for sketching a graph that shows a positive gradient.

Variant_1.question_text: Measure the distance l on Fig. 1.1 to the nearest millimetre. Record your result.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for stating l = 5.2 cm (accept answers in the range 5.1 cm to 5.3 cm).

Variant_1.question_text: Fig. 1.1 is drawn to a scale of one-eighth of the full size. Determine the actual length L of the pendulum from the point of support to the centre of the pendulum bob.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for stating L = 41.6 cm.

The following values of the time for 10 complete oscillations are recorded: 7.4 s, 7.8 s, 7.7 s. Calculate T10, the average value of the time for 10 complete oscillations. Give your answer to 2 significant figures.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for calculating the average as 7.63333 s. Award 1 mark for giving the answer as 7.6 s to 2 significant figures.

Variant_1.question_text: Explain why the student measures the time for 10 complete oscillations, rather than for 1 complete oscillation.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for stating that one oscillation is too quick to measure accurately, or that measuring time for 10 oscillations allows for a more precise average time.

Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award up to 4 marks for: 1 mark for correct plotting of points; 1 mark for appropriate scales; 1 mark for labelling axes; 1 mark for drawing a straight line of best fit.

Variant_1.question_text: Determine the gradient G of your line. Show your working and indicate on the graph the values you use.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for correct method of calculating gradient. Award 1 mark for the correct value of G.

Variant_1.question_text: Theory suggests that the gravitational field strength g is equal to: g = 0.39 / G. Use this equation and your value of G in (b)(v) to calculate a value for g.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for substituting the value of G into the equation and calculating g correctly.

Variant_1.question_text: Compare the value of g you obtained in (b)(vi) with the accepted value of 10 N/kg. State whether the two values agree with each other and justify your answer.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for stating whether the values agree (e.g., if calculated g is close to 10 N/kg, state they agree; otherwise, disagree) and providing a justification based on the difference (e.g., if the difference is small, justify as within experimental error).

Variant_1.question_text: Record the mass m of the block, correct to one decimal place.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for stating m = 29.2 g (based on the figure showing 29.165 g, rounded to one decimal place).

Variant_1.question_text: Record the volume V of water shown in Fig. 2.2.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for stating V = 35 cm cubed (reading from the figure between 30 and 40 cm cubed mark).

Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for substituting values into the equation c = [V × 4.2 × (27 – θ1)] / [m × (100 – 27)] and calculating correctly. Award 1 mark for the correct unit, which is J/g°C or equivalent.

Variant_1.question_text: Suggest one reason for this.
Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for suggesting a reason such as heat loss to the surroundings, inaccurate temperature measurement, or incomplete immersion of the block.

Variant_1.marking_scheme: Award 1 mark for correctly drawing a voltmeter in parallel with the thermistor.
